name: The Bahamas
tagline: From the Lucayan islanders and Columbus's first New World landfall, through Spanish depopulation, the pirate republic of Nassau, Loyalist plantations and emancipation, to majority rule, independence in 1973, and a modern nation of tourism, finance and hurricanes.
summary: The Bahamas is an archipelago of some seven hundred low coral islands and cays strung across the western Atlantic between Florida and Hispaniola. Its recorded history opens dramatically: it was on a Bahamian island the Lucayan people called Guanahani — usually identified with San Salvador — that Christopher Columbus made his first landfall in the Americas on 12 October 1492, after which Spanish slaving emptied the islands of their original inhabitants within a few decades. Left largely abandoned, the Bahamas drew English settlers from 1648, became a notorious pirate haven until Woodes Rogers restored Crown control in 1718, and was transformed after 1783 by American Loyalists who brought enslaved Africans to short-lived cotton plantations. Out of emancipation in the 1830s grew a majority-Black society that won majority rule in 1967 under Lynden Pindling and independence from Britain on 10 July 1973, sustaining itself today on tourism and offshore finance while contending with the ever-present threat of hurricanes such as Dorian in 2019.

founding.label: Independence from the United Kingdom
founding.detail: The Commonwealth of The Bahamas became a sovereign state on 10 July 1973, ending more than three centuries of British colonial rule. Independence came under Prime Minister Lynden Pindling and his Progressive Liberal Party, which had carried the islands to majority rule in 1967 and to internal self-government, and it followed a constitutional conference in London in December 1972. The Bahamas chose to remain within the Commonwealth as a constitutional monarchy, retaining the British sovereign as head of state represented by a governor-general, with a Westminster-style parliament at Nassau. The new nation joined the United Nations the same year. Independence crowned the "Quiet Revolution" by which the Black majority, long excluded from power by a white merchant elite, took control of their own affairs without violence.

quickFacts.label: Independence
quickFacts.value: 10 July 1973, from the United Kingdom; first prime minister Sir Lynden Pindling (PLP)

quickFacts.label: Columbus landfall
quickFacts.value: 12 October 1492, at the Lucayan island of Guanahani — usually identified as San Salvador — Columbus's first landfall in the Americas

quickFacts.label: First inhabitants
quickFacts.value: The Lucayans, a Taíno (Arawakan) people numbering perhaps 40,000, enslaved and removed by the Spanish and extinct in the islands by about 1520

quickFacts.label: Government
quickFacts.value: Commonwealth realm and parliamentary democracy; constitutional monarchy with a governor-general and a parliament at Nassau

quickFacts.label: Capital
quickFacts.value: Nassau, on New Providence — former pirate stronghold and centre of the islands' commerce and tourism

quickFacts.label: Economy
quickFacts.value: High-income economy built on tourism (around three-fifths of GDP) and offshore financial services, with no income or corporate tax

eras.title: The Lucayans and Columbus's landfall
eras.standfirst: A Taíno people, the Lucayans, settled the islands and met Columbus at Guanahani in 1492 — only to be carried off into Spanish slavery and gone within a generation.
body: The first Bahamians were the Lucayans, an Arawakan-speaking branch of the Taíno who had migrated north from Hispaniola and Cuba no later than about 700 CE and spread across the archipelago they knew as the "isles of the Lucayos". They lived in villages of thatched houses, cultivating cassava, maize and sweet potato, but drew most of their food from the sea — fish, conch and turtle — and were renowned free divers. Skilled canoe-makers and traders, they moved freely among the islands and to the Greater Antilles. By the late fifteenth century their population may have reached around forty thousand, scattered across dozens of inhabited cays in a society without metal tools or large-scale political states.

Into this world sailed Christopher Columbus. At dawn on 12 October 1492, after a five-week Atlantic crossing, his expedition made its first landfall in the Americas on an island the Lucayans called Guanahani, which Columbus renamed San Salvador and claimed for the Crown of Castile. The exact island remains debated — San Salvador (formerly Watling Island) is the traditional and most widely accepted candidate — but the event itself is fixed: it was on a Bahamian beach that the encounter between Europe and the Americas began. Columbus described the Lucayans as gentle and generous, and noted at once their potential as labourers and converts.

The consequences were catastrophic and swift. Finding no gold in the islands, the Spanish soon treated the Lucayans as a labour reserve for the mines and pearl fisheries of Hispaniola, Cuba and the Venezuelan coast. From about 1509 slave raiders systematically carried off the islanders; perhaps forty thousand were seized over the following years, and disease completed what enslavement began. When a Spanish ship went to gather the last Lucayans for removal around 1520, it reportedly found only a handful of survivors. Within roughly a generation of Columbus's landfall the Bahamas had been depopulated, and the islands lay all but empty for more than a century.

events.title: Lucayan settlement of the islands
events.summary: The Lucayans, a Taíno (Arawakan) people, migrated from Hispaniola and Cuba and settled the Bahamian archipelago, eventually numbering perhaps 40,000 across dozens of islands.

events.title: Columbus's first landfall at Guanahani
events.summary: Christopher Columbus made his first landfall in the Americas at the Lucayan island of Guanahani, which he renamed San Salvador and claimed for Castile, opening the European encounter with the New World.

events.title: Spanish enslavement of the Lucayans
events.summary: Spanish raiders began systematically seizing Lucayans as forced labour for the mines and pearl fisheries of the Greater Antilles and Venezuela, devastating the islands' population.

events.title: Depopulation of the Bahamas
events.summary: By about 1520 enslavement and disease had all but exterminated the Lucayans; the islands were left effectively abandoned for more than a century.

pullquote.text: It was on a Bahamian beach, on 12 October 1492, that the meeting of Europe and the Americas began — and within a generation the people who greeted Columbus were gone.
pullquote.attribution: On the Lucayans and the landfall

eras.title: English settlers and the Republic of Pirates
eras.standfirst: Puritan Eleutheran Adventurers brought the first lasting English settlement in 1648; by the 1700s Nassau had become a pirate haven, until Woodes Rogers seized it for the Crown in 1718.
body: The islands' long emptiness ended in 1648, when a band of English Puritans known as the Eleutheran Adventurers, led by the former Bermuda governor William Sayle, left Bermuda in search of religious freedom and settled the island they named Eleuthera, the Greek word for freedom. The colonists struggled against poor soil and shipwreck and many drifted back to Bermuda, but a foothold had been made; New Providence, with its fine harbour, was settled soon after and its town named Nassau in honour of the new English king William III. The Lords Proprietors of Carolina were granted the Bahamas in 1670, but their rule was weak and the scattered settlers scraped a living from salt-raking, fishing and salvaging wrecks.

That weakness, and Nassau's sheltered, shallow harbour — too shoal for large warships yet open to swift sloops — made the port ideal for pirates. In the political turmoil after the War of the Spanish Succession, Nassau became the headquarters of a loose "flying gang" of freebooters, the so-called Republic of Pirates that flourished from roughly 1706 to 1718. Hundreds of pirates used the island as a base to prey on Spanish, French and English shipping, among them Benjamin Hornigold, Charles Vane, Calico Jack Rackham, the women Anne Bonny and Mary Read, and the most feared of all, Edward Teach — Blackbeard. For a few years the Crown's authority in the Bahamas had effectively collapsed.

London answered by appointing a royal governor with a mandate to clear the islands. In 1718 Captain Woodes Rogers, a former privateer and circumnavigator, arrived at Nassau bearing the King's Pardon offered the previous year to any pirate who surrendered. Many accepted; those who refused, like Vane, fled or were hunted down, and Rogers fortified the harbour against a Spanish counter-attack. The colony adopted the motto "Expulsis Piratis — Restituta Commercia" ("Pirates Expelled, Commerce Restored"). Though piracy lingered for a time, Rogers's arrival is reckoned the end of the pirate republic and the beginning of stable royal government in the Bahamas.

events.title: The Eleutheran Adventurers settle Eleuthera
events.summary: English Puritans led by William Sayle left Bermuda seeking religious freedom and founded the first lasting English settlement in the Bahamas on the island they named Eleuthera ("freedom").

events.title: Grant to the Lords Proprietors of Carolina
events.summary: The Bahamas were granted to the Lords Proprietors of Carolina, though their rule proved weak and largely nominal, leaving the scattered islands poorly governed.

events.title: Nassau becomes a pirate haven
events.summary: Amid a collapse of royal authority, Nassau became the base of a loose pirate republic, home to Blackbeard, Charles Vane, Anne Bonny and others who preyed on Atlantic shipping.

events.title: Woodes Rogers ends the pirate republic
events.summary: Royal governor Woodes Rogers arrived at Nassau with the King's Pardon, accepted the surrender of many pirates, drove out the rest and restored Crown authority under the motto "Pirates Expelled, Commerce Restored".

figures.name: Woodes Rogers
figures.role: First royal Governor of the Bahamas
figures.blurb: Former privateer and circumnavigator appointed governor in 1717, who arrived at Nassau in 1718 with the King's Pardon, broke the pirate republic and restored royal government; he served two terms as governor and gave the colony its motto of commerce restored.

pullquote.text: Expulsis Piratis — Restituta Commercia: Pirates Expelled, Commerce Restored.
pullquote.attribution: Colonial motto adopted after Woodes Rogers's arrival, 1718

eras.title: Loyalists, slavery and emancipation
eras.standfirst: American Loyalists arrived after 1783 with thousands of enslaved Africans and tried to plant cotton; the experiment failed, and emancipation from the 1830s created a majority of African descent.
body: Through the eighteenth century the Bahamas remained a poor and thinly populated colony, raided by the Spanish and briefly held by them, and recovered for Britain by Loyalist forces in 1783; under the Treaty of Paris that year Spain formally ceded the islands to Britain. The colony's character was then transformed by the American Revolution. Defeated American Loyalists, unwilling to live in the new United States, emigrated to the Bahamas in large numbers after 1783, bringing with them the enslaved Africans they held. This influx roughly trebled the population, raising the number of white inhabitants to around three thousand and of enslaved people to some six thousand, and decisively shifting the islands toward a plantation society.

The Loyalists laid out cotton plantations on the so-called "Cotton Islands" — Cat Island, the Exumas, Long Island, Crooked Island, San Salvador and Rum Cay. For a few years cotton flourished, but the thin Bahamian soils were quickly exhausted by the slash-and-burn methods used to clear them, and the chenille bug and other pests ruined the crop. By the early nineteenth century the plantation experiment had largely collapsed, and many planters abandoned their estates, leaving the enslaved population to subsistence farming. The enslaved Africans of the Bahamas thus came, in time, to greatly outnumber the white minority.

Britain abolished the slave trade in 1807, and the Royal Navy thereafter landed thousands of "liberated Africans" — people freed from intercepted slave ships — in the Bahamas, adding to the population of African descent. The Slavery Abolition Act took effect across the British Caribbean on 1 August 1834, beginning the end of slavery itself; after a transitional period of "apprenticeship", full emancipation came in 1838. The freed people of the Bahamas turned to small farming, fishing, sponging and salt-raking. Though political and economic power remained in the hands of a small white elite for more than a century afterward, the foundations of a majority-Black nation had been laid.

events.title: Spain cedes the Bahamas to Britain
events.summary: Under the Treaty of Paris of 1783 Spain ceded the Bahamas to Britain, securing the colony's future under the Crown after years of contest and a brief Spanish occupation.

events.title: American Loyalists and enslaved Africans arrive
events.summary: Loyalist refugees from the new United States emigrated to the Bahamas after the American Revolution, bringing thousands of enslaved Africans and roughly trebling the colony's population.

events.title: The failed cotton plantations
events.summary: Loyalists planted cotton on the "Cotton Islands", but exhausted soils and pests ruined the crop within a couple of decades, collapsing the plantation economy.

events.title: Slavery Abolition Act takes effect
events.summary: The British Slavery Abolition Act came into force in the Bahamas on 1 August 1834, beginning emancipation; after an apprenticeship period, full freedom followed in 1838.

pullquote.text: The Loyalists' cotton failed within a generation, but the people they brought in bondage — and those the navy later freed — became the ancestors of the Bahamian nation.
pullquote.attribution: On the plantation era and emancipation

eras.title: Wrecking, blockade-running and rum
eras.standfirst: A poor colony living off salvage and sponging found sudden riches twice — running the Union blockade in the American Civil War, and smuggling liquor to Prohibition-era America.
body: For most of the nineteenth century the post-emancipation Bahamas was a poor and forgotten colony of a few thousand people, surviving on subsistence farming, sponge-fishing, salt-raking and, above all, "wrecking" — the salvaging of cargo and ships from vessels foundering on the treacherous Bahamian reefs. Wrecking was for a time the islands' single largest source of income, and Nassau's merchants grew expert at handling salvaged goods, false papers and the quiet movement of cargo. That maritime cunning would twice make the colony's fortune when larger powers went to war.

The first windfall came with the American Civil War. After 1861 the Union blockade of the Confederate coast turned Nassau, lying close to the Carolinas and Georgia, into the great transshipment hub for blockade-running. Fast, shallow-draught steamers carried British arms, munitions and luxuries in to the South and brought out cotton, and the trade made Nassau spectacularly rich: warehouses overflowed, fortunes were made, and grand buildings such as the Royal Victoria Hotel rose on the proceeds. When the Confederacy collapsed in 1865 the boom ended as abruptly as it had begun, and Nassau sank back into depression.

The second windfall came with American Prohibition. From 1920, when the United States banned the sale of alcohol, the Bahamas — and Nassau, Bimini and Grand Bahama in particular — became a base for "rum-running": the smuggling of whisky, champagne and gin across the Gulf Stream to a thirsty American market. Liquor revenues poured into the colonial treasury and into the pockets of Bay Street merchants and bootleggers. When Prohibition was repealed in 1933, that revenue vanished, and the near-simultaneous collapse of the sponge beds from disease left the Bahamas once again economically prostrate on the eve of the tourist age.

events.title: An economy of wrecking and sponging
events.summary: The poor post-emancipation colony lived largely on salvaging shipwrecks, sponge-fishing and salt, with wrecking for a time its single largest source of income.

events.title: Civil War blockade-running boom
events.summary: During the American Civil War, Nassau became the chief hub for running the Union blockade, shipping arms to the Confederacy and cotton out, and growing fabulously wealthy in the process.

events.title: Collapse of the blockade boom
events.summary: The defeat of the Confederacy in 1865 ended blockade-running overnight and plunged Nassau back into economic depression.

events.title: Prohibition-era rum-running
events.summary: American Prohibition turned the Bahamas into a base for smuggling liquor across the Gulf Stream, enriching the colony until repeal in 1933 ended the trade.

pullquote.text: Twice a poor colony of salvagers struck it rich on other nations' laws — running the Union blockade, then running rum to a dry America.
pullquote.attribution: On the Bahamas' boom-and-bust nineteenth and early twentieth centuries

eras.title: Majority rule and independence
eras.standfirst: Tourism revived the islands while a Black majority, long shut out by the "Bay Street Boys", organised through the PLP to win majority rule in 1967 and independence in 1973.
body: As Prohibition's profits faded, the modern Bahamian economy began to take shape around tourism, drawing American visitors to Nassau and, after the Second World War, to new resorts and casinos. But political and economic power stayed firmly with a small white elite of Nassau merchants known as the "Bay Street Boys", who dominated the legislature and the economy under the banner of the United Bahamian Party (UBP). The Black majority, the descendants of the enslaved and of liberated Africans, were largely excluded from real power despite forming the great bulk of the population.

Their challenge was organised through the Progressive Liberal Party, founded in 1953, which became the vehicle of the movement for majority rule. A pivotal general strike in 1958 and the slow widening of the franchise — universal adult suffrage came in stages, with women gaining the vote in 1962 — eroded the old order. The Bahamas attained internal self-government under a new constitution on 7 January 1964, with the UBP's Roland Symonette as first premier, and a further constitution in 1969 advanced it toward full self-rule. The decisive moment came on 10 January 1967, when the PLP, led by the lawyer Lynden Pindling, won enough seats to form a government and Pindling became the colony's first Black premier — the bloodless "Quiet Revolution" that brought majority rule.

Pindling's government then moved toward sovereignty. The PLP won a commanding victory in the 1972 election on a platform of independence, a constitutional conference met in London that December, and at midnight on 9–10 July 1973 the Union Flag was lowered and the Commonwealth of The Bahamas became an independent state, with Pindling as its first prime minister. The new nation kept the British monarch as head of state, represented by a Bahamian governor-general, and joined the United Nations and the Commonwealth. Power had passed, without bloodshed, from a colonial elite to the country's Black majority.

events.title: Founding of the Progressive Liberal Party
events.summary: Bahamians opposed to UBP and "Bay Street" dominance founded the Progressive Liberal Party, which became the vehicle of the movement for majority rule.

events.title: Internal self-government
events.summary: A new constitution granted the Bahamas internal self-government with a cabinet and a first premier, while Britain retained defence and external affairs.

events.title: Majority rule — the Quiet Revolution
events.summary: Lynden Pindling's PLP gained power after the 1967 election, making him the first Black premier and ending the long political dominance of the white "Bay Street" elite.

events.title: Independence of The Bahamas
events.summary: The Commonwealth of The Bahamas became independent of the United Kingdom on 10 July 1973 under Prime Minister Lynden Pindling, remaining a Commonwealth realm and joining the United Nations.

figures.name: Sir Lynden Pindling
figures.role: First Prime Minister of The Bahamas
figures.blurb: Lawyer and leader of the Progressive Liberal Party who led the Bahamas to majority rule in 1967 as its first Black premier and to independence in 1973, then governed as prime minister until 1992; knighted in 1983, he is widely regarded as the "Father of the Nation".

pullquote.text: On 10 January 1967 a bloodless "Quiet Revolution" passed power to the Black majority; six years later the Bahamas were free.

eras.title: Tourism, finance and the age of hurricanes
eras.standfirst: An independent Bahamas built a prosperous economy on tourism and offshore finance, alternated power peacefully between rival parties, and confronted catastrophic storms such as Hurricane Dorian in 2019.
body: Independence opened a long era of stable parliamentary democracy and growing prosperity. Lynden Pindling and the PLP governed for nineteen years after 1973, presiding over the build-out of mass tourism and a booming offshore financial-services industry that took advantage of the country's lack of income, capital-gains and corporate taxes. In 1992 power passed peacefully for the first time, when Hubert Ingraham's Free National Movement defeated the PLP, ending Pindling's quarter-century in office. Since then government has alternated between the two parties — Ingraham and the FNM, Perry Christie and Hubert Minnis, and from 2021 Philip Davis of the PLP — in regular, peaceful elections, confirming the Bahamas as one of the Caribbean's more durable democracies.

The modern Bahamian economy rests on two pillars. Tourism — beaches, cruise ships, resorts and casinos drawing millions of mostly American visitors — generates roughly three-fifths of GDP and employs a large share of the workforce, making the Bahamas the most tourism-dependent economy in the Caribbean. Offshore banking, trust and insurance services add a further substantial slice, sustained by the country's tax regime and proximity to the United States, though they have brought scrutiny over money-laundering and tax transparency. The combination has given the Bahamas one of the higher per-capita incomes in the Americas, with the World Bank classing it among high-income economies.

That prosperity is shadowed by the sea that made it. Lying low across hurricane alley, the Bahamas is acutely exposed to tropical storms, and in early September 2019 the most powerful on record struck: Hurricane Dorian, a Category 5 storm, stalled for days over Abaco and Grand Bahama, killing scores of people, leaving hundreds missing and causing damage estimated at some 3.4 billion US dollars — about a quarter of the country's annual output. Reconstruction, climate resilience and the threat of rising seas now sit alongside tourism and finance at the centre of national life. More than three centuries after Columbus, the low coral islands he first sighted form a sovereign, prosperous and storm-battered nation of around four hundred thousand people.

events.title: First peaceful change of government
events.summary: Hubert Ingraham's Free National Movement defeated the PLP in 1992, ending Lynden Pindling's twenty-five years in power in the country's first democratic alternation of government.

events.title: Hurricane Dorian devastates Abaco and Grand Bahama
events.summary: Category 5 Hurricane Dorian stalled over the northern Bahamas, killing scores, leaving hundreds missing and causing about US$3.4 billion in damage — roughly a quarter of GDP.

events.title: Philip Davis and the PLP return to power
events.summary: Philip Davis led the Progressive Liberal Party to victory in the 2021 general election, becoming prime minister, in another peaceful transfer of power.

figures.name: Hubert Ingraham
figures.blurb: Lawyer and Free National Movement leader who in 1992 ended Lynden Pindling's twenty-five-year rule in the first democratic change of government, and served two further terms as prime minister, presiding over continued growth in tourism and finance.

pullquote.text: More than three centuries after Columbus, the low coral islands he first sighted form a sovereign, prosperous and storm-battered nation.

figures.name: Sir Lynden Pindling
figures.role: First Prime Minister of The Bahamas
figures.blurb: Leader of the Progressive Liberal Party who brought the Bahamas to majority rule in 1967 as its first Black premier and to independence in 1973, then governed as prime minister until 1992; knighted in 1983 and remembered as the "Father of the Nation".

figures.name: Woodes Rogers
figures.role: First royal Governor of the Bahamas
figures.blurb: Privateer and circumnavigator who arrived at Nassau in 1718 with the King's Pardon, broke up the pirate republic and restored Crown government to the islands.

figures.name: Hubert Ingraham
figures.blurb: Free National Movement leader who ended the PLP's twenty-five-year rule in 1992 and served three terms in all, overseeing the maturing of the tourism and offshore-finance economy.
